cues.x: Mindset, by Carol S. Dweck.
Let me start with a small scene, because this whole book really did begin with a small scene.
Picture a psychologist watching a group of ten year olds work on a stack of puzzles.
The puzzles are designed to get harder as the children go, so that eventually every child hits a wall.
And the psychologist, Carol Dweck, expected the same thing you would probably expect.
She thought the children would fall into two groups: the ones who could cope with difficulty and the ones who could not.
She thought some kids would keep their spirits up and some would lose heart.
That is not what she saw.
What she saw genuinely shocked her, and it reoriented her entire career.
As the puzzles got harder, one boy pulled his chair up closer, rubbed his hands together, smacked his lips, and said, "I love a challenge."
Another child, sweating over a problem he clearly could not solve yet, looked up with real satisfaction and said, "You know, I was hoping this would be informative."
Dweck sat there thinking, what is wrong with these children?
Or maybe, what is right with them, that I have never understood?
Because these kids were not just coping with failure.
They did not even seem to think they were failing.
They thought they were learning.
And that tiny gap, the gap between a child who sees a hard problem as a threat and a child who sees a hard problem as a gift, turned out to be one of the most important gaps in all of psychology.
This book is Dweck's attempt to explain that gap, where it comes from, what it does to a life, and how you can move yourself and the people you love from one side of it to the other.
So here is the core idea, the single distinction that the entire book rests on.
Dweck says people carry around a belief about the nature of their own abilities, and most of us are not even aware we hold it.
Some people quietly believe that their qualities, their intelligence, their talent, their character, are basically fixed.
You get a certain hand of cards at birth, and life is about proving that your hand is a good one.
Dweck calls this the fixed mindset.
If you live in the fixed mindset, every situation becomes a kind of test.
Am I smart or dumb?
Am I a winner or a loser?
Will I look talented or will I look like a fraud?
Because if your abilities are fixed, then every failure is not just a failure at a task.
It is a verdict on you.
It measures the fixed amount of stuff you were given, and it comes back and tells you that you do not have enough.
The other belief is that your basic qualities are things you can cultivate through effort, through good strategies, and through help from other people.
Not that everyone is the same, not that anyone can be Einstein, but that everyone can grow and change with work.
Dweck calls this the growth mindset.
If you live in the growth mindset, a hard task is not a referendum on your worth.
It is an opportunity to get better at something.
Failure still hurts, but it does not define you.
It informs you.
That is why those ten year olds could look at an impossible puzzle and feel curious instead of ashamed.
They were not measuring themselves.
They were stretching themselves.
Now, I want to be careful here, because it is easy to hear this and think it is just a pep talk, just the power of positive thinking with a new coat of paint.
It is not.
Dweck spent decades running careful experiments, and the findings are specific and sometimes startling.
Let me walk you through the one that, more than any other, made people sit up.
Dweck and her colleagues went into New York City schools and worked with hundreds of fifth graders, children around ten and eleven years old.
They gave each child a set of fairly easy puzzles, and nearly all the kids did well.
Then, and this is the whole experiment, they praised the children.
But they praised different children in different ways.
Some children got praised for their ability.
The researcher would say something like, "Wow, you got a really high score.
You must be smart at this."
Other children got praised for their effort.
The researcher would say, "Wow, you got a really high score.
You must have worked really hard."
That was the only difference.
One sentence.
Smart versus worked hard.
Then they watched what that single sentence did.
First they offered every child a choice for the next round.
You can do a harder puzzle that you will learn a lot from, or you can do an easy one like the one you just did.
The children who had been praised for being smart mostly chose the easy puzzle.
They did not want to risk it.
They had just been told they were smart, and a hard puzzle might expose them, so they played it safe.
The children praised for effort overwhelmingly chose the hard puzzle.
They wanted the one they could learn from.
Same kids, moments earlier, one sentence apart.
Then the researchers gave everyone a genuinely hard round, too hard for fifth graders, so that every child struggled.
And the two groups reacted to struggle in completely opposite ways.
The kids praised for intelligence took the struggle as proof they were not so smart after all.
They stopped enjoying it.
They looked miserable.
The kids praised for effort tended to say the hard problems were their favorite.
They rolled up their sleeves.
They stayed engaged.
And here is the kicker: when the researchers gave a final round, back at the original easy level, the intelligence-praised children actually did worse than they had at the very start.
Being told they were smart had made them fragile, and one hard round knocked their scores down.
The effort-praised children improved.
Their scores went up.
The praise for effort had made them more resilient and, in the end, more capable.
And then the detail that people never forget.
The researchers asked the children to write a note to a child at another school, describing the experiment, and there was a line to report their own score.
Among the children who had been praised for being smart, a striking number, close to four in ten, lied.
They inflated their scores.
Ten year olds who had been told, one time, that they were smart, were already willing to fib to a stranger to protect that label.
Because in a fixed mindset, your score is not information.
It is your identity.
And you will bend the truth to keep the identity safe.

Dweck's conclusion was uncomfortable and important: praising children for their intelligence, the most natural, loving thing in the world, can quietly teach them to fear challenge, to wilt under difficulty, and to lie about failure.
It hands them a fixed mindset.
Praising the effort and the process hands them a growth mindset.
Same child.
Different future.
You might wonder whether these mindsets are just a story people tell, or whether something is actually different in the brain.
So let me tell you about a study done at Columbia, where researchers hooked people up to measure their brain waves while they answered hard questions and then got feedback.
What they found maps beautifully onto the two mindsets.
For the people with a fixed mindset, their brains lit up with attention at one particular moment: when they were told whether their answer was right or wrong.
That was the part they cared about.
Am I right?
Did I pass?
But then, when the correct answer was presented, the part that would actually let them learn, their attention went flat.
They tuned out the information.
They only wanted the grade.
The people with a growth mindset showed the opposite pattern.
Their attention spiked when the correct answer and the explanation came, because that was the part that would make them better next time.
One group is wired, at least in that moment, to protect a self image.
The other is wired to learn.
And Dweck's point is that this wiring is not fixed either.
It follows the belief.
Change the belief and the attention follows.

Now let me take this out of the lab, because the reason this book became so beloved is that once you have the two mindsets in your head, you start seeing them everywhere, in sports, in business, in love, in parenting, in yourself.
Let us start with sports, because sports is the one place where we most stubbornly believe in the natural, the god-given talent, the person who was simply born to it.
Consider Michael Jordan, who by the end was treated as if he had descended from basketball heaven.
But as a sophomore he was cut from his high school varsity team.
Cut.
He went home, shut himself in his room, and cried.
And then what did he do?
He practiced.
He would leave the house at six in the morning to practice before school.
When he was cut, his mother said he should go back and discipline himself, and he did.
Through college and into the pros, he kept doing the thing fixed mindset geniuses do not do: he worked relentlessly on his weaknesses.
When people said he was a natural, they were erasing the most important thing about him, which was the labor.
Jordan himself said he succeeded because he failed over and over.
That is the growth mindset wearing a jersey.
Now hold Jordan next to John McEnroe, who was, by raw talent, extraordinary, and who tells you in his own memoirs the whole psychology of the fixed mindset.
When McEnroe won, it was because he was a genius.
When McEnroe lost, it was never because someone outplayed him or because he needed to grow.
It was the sawdust on the floor that was the wrong kind.
It was his racquet.
It was the crowd.
It was a cold he had, a bad night, an unfair situation.

He reached number one and, by his own account, did not love the game and did not really improve, because in his mind he had already arrived at fixed greatness and everything after was about defending it.

Dweck's contrast is devastating and fair: two immensely talented men, and the one with the growth mindset kept getting better and became a byword for excellence, while the one guarding a fixed identity spent his gifts blaming the sawdust.
Think about Muhammad Ali.
If you measured Ali the way boxing scouts measured fighters, his physical numbers were unremarkable.
His reach, his chest, the standard measurements, they did not mark him as a superior specimen.
He beat Sonny Liston, a fearsome puncher, not with superior raw power but with study.
Ali studied Liston as a person, not just a fighter.
He worked out how to get inside his head, how to make him emotional, how to control the psychology of the fight.
He won with his mind.

Dweck's gallery of growth mindset athletes is full of people like this: Mia Hamm, who said that all her life she was playing up, against people older, bigger, more skilled, and chose to, because that is how you get better; Jackie Joyner-Kersee, who found joy in the process; Wilma Rudolph, who overcame a childhood of illness and a leg brace to become the fastest woman in the world and who never described herself as a natural.
And out of all this Dweck pulls a word we usually use loosely: character.
What is character, in athletic terms?

She says it is the ability to reach deep down and produce your best precisely when things are going worst, when you are losing, when the pressure is crushing, when the easy story is that you simply are not good enough today.
Fixed mindset athletes, when it goes badly, tend to fold, because the bad moment feels like the truth about them.
Growth mindset athletes have something to reach for, because to them the bad moment is just a problem to be solved, and solving problems is what they do.
Let us move from the locker room to the boardroom, because the same two mindsets run entire companies up or into the ground.
Dweck tells the story of Enron, the energy company whose spectacular collapse became a symbol of corporate rot.
She argues that at the heart of Enron was a mindset, imported from a certain strain of consulting culture, that she calls the talent mindset.
The idea was that the company's job was to hire the smartest people, the stars, the geniuses, and then worship them.
Sounds reasonable.
But watch what it does.

If your whole identity inside the company is being the smartest person in the room, then admitting a mistake, admitting you are lost, admitting a deal is going bad, becomes impossible, because it would puncture the one thing that makes you valuable.
So the smartest people in the room could not say the three most important words in business, which are I was wrong, or the ones right behind them, I need help.
A culture built on proving genius becomes a culture that cannot face reality, and a company that cannot face reality eventually meets it all at once.
Now put beside Enron a single executive, Lee Iacocca, who took over a struggling Chrysler and, for a while, was a genuine hero.
He rescued the company, brought out popular cars, became a celebrity, wrote a bestselling memoir.
And then, Dweck argues, the fixed mindset caught up with him.
Iacocca became consumed with his own image, his own legend, his standing as a great man.
He reportedly cared enormously about how he stacked up, about status symbols, about being celebrated.

And as the world changed and Japanese carmakers surged ahead, Iacocca could not hear the bad news, could not adapt, spent energy on his legacy and on settling scores rather than on the humble, unglamorous work of building better cars.
The very fixed mindset that made him need to be a hero made him unable to keep being one.
Against these, Dweck lines up a group of leaders who ran their companies with a growth mindset, and the contrast is the whole argument.
Jack Welch at General Electric is her centerpiece.
We remember Welch as a tough, larger than life boss, but Dweck's Welch is something more interesting.
He prized learning over ego.
He roamed the company talking to people at every level, not to be adored but to find out what he did not know.
He famously hired for growth and for people who could develop, rather than for pedigree alone.
And here is the detail that tells you everything about his values.
Welch judged managers on two axes: did they hit their numbers, and did they live the company's values.
And he made a point of removing managers who hit their numbers but violated the values, the ones who got results by bullying, by taking credit, by protecting their own image at others' expense.
In a fixed mindset company you keep the jerk who delivers.
Welch fired him, because he understood that the how matters as much as the what, and that a culture of image protection eventually eats the results too.
She gives you more of these.
Lou Gerstner, who took over a dying IBM and turned it around not with swagger but by tearing down the arrogance and the internal politics, by listening, by rebuilding a culture that could learn.

Anne Mulcahy, who inherited a Xerox on the edge of bankruptcy and, instead of pretending she had all the answers, admitted what she did not know, learned the business in brutal detail, made painful honest choices, and pulled the company back from the brink.
Darwin Smith, who ran Kimberly-Clark for decades and made bold, humble, unglamorous decisions that transformed it, all while being a modest man nobody would have picked out of a crowd.

And Dweck connects this directly to the research of Jim Collins, who studied companies that made the leap from good to truly great and found that they were almost all led by a certain kind of person he called a Level 5 leader.
These leaders were a strange blend of intense professional will and deep personal humility.
They were not the swaggering, magazine cover celebrity CEOs.
They were people who took blame when things went wrong and gave credit when things went right, who cared about the enterprise more than their own glory.
In other words, they were growth mindset leaders, and Collins found them, independently, at the top of the most enduring companies.
Along the way Dweck names two related traps that grow in the fixed mindset soil.

One is groupthink, where a team, often around a charismatic leader, stops disagreeing, stops raising problems, and starts agreeing with each other into disaster, because dissent feels like disloyalty and no one wants to be the person who looks negative or stupid.

The other she calls CEO disease, the tendency of powerful people, once they are up on a pedestal, to become addicted to the pedestal, to surround themselves with flattery, to punish bad news, and so to lose contact with the truth their organization desperately needs them to hear.
Both are what happens when the person at the top is spending their energy proving they are a genius instead of trying to learn.
Now let us bring the two mindsets home, into your relationships, because this might be where they cut the closest.
Dweck argues that the fixed mindset carries a set of beliefs about love that quietly poison it.
The first is the belief that if a relationship is meant to be, it should be effortless.
That true love means you never have to work at it, that compatibility means friction free.

So the moment a real relationship requires effort, requires talking through a hard thing, requires changing a habit, the fixed mindset person concludes that this must not be the right relationship, because the right one would not be this hard.
The second belief is that your partner should be able to read your mind, that if they really loved you and really got you, you would not have to explain what you need, they would just know.
And of course no one can actually do that, so this belief guarantees a steady supply of disappointment.

The third is the belief that conflict itself is a sign that something is fundamentally wrong, that a good relationship is one without problems, rather than one where two people are good at solving problems together.
You can hear how all three come from the same root.
If people and relationships are fixed, then problems reveal flaws that cannot be fixed, so problems are terrifying and must mean doom.

If people and relationships can grow, then problems are just the normal friction of two real humans building something, and the work of resolving them is not a sign the love is failing, it is the very substance of the love.
Dweck extends this to how we handle rejection, which is one of the most painful human experiences.
In a fixed mindset, rejection is a verdict.

It says something permanent and terrible about your worth, and so it can curdle into lasting bitterness, into a need to prove the other person wrong, into a fantasy of revenge, into an inability to move on because moving on would mean accepting the verdict.

In a growth mindset, rejection still hurts, badly, but it can eventually become information, something you learn from, something that, without excusing the pain, points you toward growth and toward forgiveness, so that you are not chained to the person who hurt you.
Dweck also turns this lens on something that touches almost every childhood: bullying.
She describes bullying as, at its core, an act of judgment.
The bully is asserting a fixed hierarchy of human worth and placing themselves on top by placing someone else on the bottom.
Bullies often pick targets and then feel powerful and superior in the judging.

And the deepest damage to victims comes when they accept the verdict, when they internalize the bully's fixed mindset judgment and conclude that they really are less, that there is something permanently wrong with them.

Some of the most tragic cases, the ones that end in violence turned inward or outward, come from victims who bought the fixed mindset story completely, who came to believe the ranking was true and permanent.

Dweck's hope here is not naive, but it is real: teaching both the bullied and even the bullies a growth mindset, the idea that worth is not fixed and that people can change, takes away some of the terrible power of the judgment.
So where do these mindsets come from?
A big part of the answer is the messages we get, especially as children, and this brings us to parents, teachers, and coaches, and to what may be the most practical single lesson in the whole book.
Remember the fifth graders.
The lesson is: praise the process, not the person.
When a child succeeds, our instinct is to say you are so smart, you are so talented, you are a natural, what a genius.
It feels like love, and it is meant as love.
But Dweck's research says these are traps, because they all praise a fixed trait.

They teach the child that what you value, what makes them worthy in your eyes, is a fixed thing they either have or do not, and now they have to keep proving they have it, which makes them afraid of anything that might show they do not.

The alternative is to praise the process: the strategy they used, the effort they put in, the focus they kept, the persistence when it got hard, the choices they made, the way they tried a different approach when the first one failed.
Not empty praise for flailing, but genuine attention to how they got there.
Praise like that teaches a child that these things, effort and strategy and persistence, are what produce success, and those are things anyone can do more of.
You are literally teaching them the growth mindset with your sentences.
And Dweck adds one small word that carries enormous weight: yet.
When a child says I cannot do this, the fixed mindset hears a permanent fact.
The growth mindset adds one word: you cannot do this yet.
That word turns a closed door into a hallway.
She tells of a high school that did something quietly brilliant: instead of giving failing students a grade of Fail, they gave a grade of Not Yet.
Fail says you are, at the end of the road, found wanting.
Not Yet says you are on the road, and you are not at the destination, but the destination still exists and you are still moving toward it.
Same academic reality.
Completely different message about who the student is and what is possible.
Then Dweck shows you what happens when a great teacher lives this all the way.

She tells the story of Marva Collins, who taught in inner city Chicago, in classrooms full of children that the wider system had written off, some labeled unteachable, some already treated as failures at age six.
Collins did two things at once that the fixed mindset says are contradictory.

She held these children to standards that seemed impossibly high, second graders reading Shakespeare and Tolstoy and tackling material college students find hard, and at the same time she radiated a fierce, unshakable belief that they could meet those standards.
She would tell them, in effect, I love you, I will not let you fail, you can do the hard thing, and we are going to do it together.
She was demanding and warm at the same time, and the combination transformed children everyone else had given up on.
That is the growth mindset in a classroom: high standards plus deep belief plus the message that ability grows.
She gives you another, Rafe Esquith, a teacher in Los Angeles who worked with poor, immigrant, at risk kids and got extraordinary results, again with Shakespeare, with music, with mathematics.
Esquith had a motto that hung over everything he did, and it is the whole philosophy in four words: there are no shortcuts.
Not there is no hope, and not you were born wanting.

Just that greatness comes from doing the work, day after day, without cheating the process, and that any child willing to do that work can get somewhere they and everyone around them thought was out of reach.

And she brings in the great coach John Wooden, whose teaching was all about preparation, about doing the small things right, about competing against your own potential rather than against an image, about the daily unglamorous cultivation of skill and character.
Underneath these stories sits a piece of research that should make every parent exhale.

Benjamin Bloom studied a large group of world class achievers, people who had reached the very top in fields from music to mathematics to athletics to science, and he went back and looked for the early signs of exceptional, innate talent, the childhood genius we assume such people must have shown.
And in most of them, he could not find it.
Most of these world class people did not look remarkably gifted as children.
What they had was years and years of nurturing, teaching, encouragement, and above all their own sustained effort.
Bloom's blunt conclusion was that, apart from a few extreme constraints, what any person can learn, almost all people can learn, if they are given the right conditions.
The extraordinary adult was not an extraordinary child.
The extraordinary adult was a person who was helped and who worked.
Dweck and her colleagues did not just study all this and describe it.
They tried to change it, and this is one of the most hopeful parts of the book.
They built a program, later developed into something called Brainology, that teaches students directly about the brain.
The central message is simple and, importantly, true: the brain is like a muscle.
When you struggle with something hard and stick with it, the neurons form new and stronger connections, and over time you literally get smarter.
The brain is not a fixed lump of gray matter you were issued at birth.
It grows with use.
And when students learn this, when they are shown that struggling at the edge of their ability is exactly what makes the brain grow, something shifts.
Kids who were sliding, who had checked out, who thought they were just not math people or just not smart, start to engage, because now the struggle means something different.
It is not evidence that they are dumb.
It is the feeling of their brain getting stronger.
Just teaching children how learning actually works, at the level of the brain, moves them toward the growth mindset and lifts their motivation and their grades.

Now I want to slow down and give you a nuance that Dweck herself stressed later, after the book became famous, because the idea got so popular that it also got flattened and misunderstood, and she wanted to rescue it.
She calls the flattened version the false growth mindset.
Here is the trap.
People heard growth mindset and reduced it to one word: effort.
They thought it meant you just praise effort, you tell everyone to try hard, you put up a poster that says never give up, and you are done.
And that is not it.
Effort is essential, but effort by itself is not the growth mindset, and this is crucial.

If a child is trying hard, banging their head against the same wall with the same approach, and getting nowhere, praising the effort is not kindness, it is a consolation prize, and children see through it instantly.
The growth mindset is not just about effort.
It is about learning.
It is about effort combined with new strategies, and asking for help, and trying a different way when the current way is not working.
Effort is a means to learning, not the point.
When effort is not working, the growth mindset does not say try harder at the same thing.
It says try differently, get help, find another route.
Rewarding effort that leads nowhere, and calling that growth mindset, is exactly the kind of empty praise Dweck warned against in the first place.
She adds a second nuance that is just as important and even more humbling.
Nobody is purely one mindset.
It is tempting to read this book and decide you are a growth mindset person, one of the good ones, and to go around diagnosing the fixed mindset people around you.
But that itself is a fixed mindset move, sorting people into permanent boxes.
The truth is that everyone is a mixture.
We all have both mindsets in us, and they are domain specific and situationally triggered.
You might have a beautiful growth mindset about your work, embracing every challenge, and a rigidly fixed mindset about your artistic ability, or your athletic ability, or your capacity to be loved.
And your fixed mindset gets triggered by particular things: by a big challenge, by criticism, by watching someone else succeed at what you want to do, by a setback.

In those moments a fixed mindset voice shows up in your head, sometimes the voice of a persona Dweck says many of us carry, a voice that says do not risk it, do not try, if you fail everyone will know you are not good enough, make an excuse, protect yourself.
The goal is not to declare yourself a growth mindset person and be done.
The goal is to notice when your fixed mindset shows up and to learn to work with it.
Which brings us to the most practical framework in the book, the part you can actually use, a four step method for changing your mindset in the moments that matter.
It goes like this.
Step one: learn to hear your fixed mindset voice.
Start paying attention to the voice in your head when you face a challenge, hit a setback, or get criticized.
Listen for the fixed mindset talking.
It says things like, are you sure you can do this, maybe you do not have the talent.
It says, if you do not try, you can protect yourself and keep your dignity.
When you fail it says, this is your fault, and it means what you feared, that you are not good enough.
When you are criticized it says, it is not my fault, and it gets defensive and blames.
You cannot do anything about this voice until you can hear it clearly and recognize it for what it is, one interpretation of events, not the truth.
Step two: recognize that you have a choice.
This is the pivot the whole method turns on.
How you interpret challenges, setbacks, and criticism is your choice.
That fixed mindset voice is offering you a story, a story where your abilities are on trial and the safest thing is to protect your image.
But you do not have to accept that story.
There is another way to interpret the exact same events, through the growth mindset, and you get to choose which lens you look through.
Just knowing that it is a choice, and not simply reality, gives you back your power.
Step three: talk back to the fixed mindset voice with a growth mindset voice.
When the fixed voice says maybe you do not have the talent, the growth voice answers, I am not sure I can do it now, but I think I can learn to with time and effort.
When the fixed voice says what if you fail, you will be a failure, the growth voice answers, most successful people failed on the way, failure is how you learn.

When the fixed voice, after a setback, says it is not my fault, something got in my way, the growth voice answers, if I do not take responsibility, I cannot fix it, so let me listen and learn, however painful that is.
You are not silencing the fixed voice, which may always be there.
You are answering it, out loud in your mind, with the truth.
Step four, and this is the one that actually changes your life: take the growth mindset action.
It is not enough to think growth mindset thoughts.

Over time, the way you keep a growth mindset is by acting on it: taking on the challenge you were afraid of, learning from the setback instead of hiding from it, hearing the criticism and using it, sticking with the hard thing and trying new strategies until you break through.
The action feeds the belief, and the belief feeds the next action.
Let me make this concrete with the kind of example Dweck uses, because in the abstract it can sound easy and it is not.
Imagine you have poured months into something, a manuscript, a design, a proposal, and you send it out, and it comes back rejected, with a page of criticism.
The fixed mindset voice arrives instantly and it is loud.

It says, see, you were never really good at this, who did you think you were, they can tell you are a fraud, the safest thing now is to quit so you never have to feel this again, and by the way the person who rejected you clearly has no taste and is probably wrong.
That is step one, and just naming that voice, saying out loud, ah, there is my fixed mindset, already loosens its grip.
Step two, you remind yourself that this is an interpretation and you have a choice about how to read this rejection.

Step three, you answer it: this rejection is painful, but it is not a verdict on my worth, it is information about this piece of work, and buried in that harsh page of criticism there are probably two or three things that are actually true and would make the work better.

Step four, the hard part, you do something: you sit down and read the criticism again, slowly, looking for what is useful, you ask someone you trust for help seeing your blind spots, you revise with new strategies rather than just gritting your teeth and resubmitting the same thing harder, and you send it out again.
Same rejection, same painful page.
A fixed mindset turns it into an ending.
A growth mindset turns it into a draft.

Or picture a hard conversation you have been avoiding, with a partner or a colleague, because every time you imagine it, the fixed mindset says, if you bring this up it will prove the relationship, or the job, is broken, and conflict means failure, and anyway they should already know how you feel.
Hear that voice.
Recognize you have a choice about what the conversation means.

Answer back: raising a hard thing is not evidence that this is broken, it is how two people who are committed to each other actually build something that lasts, and I cannot expect anyone to read my mind.
Then take the action: have the conversation, kindly and honestly, treating it as a problem the two of you can solve together rather than a trial where somebody has to be found guilty.
That is the growth mindset, not as a slogan on a poster, but as a thing you do on a difficult Tuesday.

So let me pull all of this together into what you might actually change, starting Monday morning, depending on who you are, because Dweck is emphatic that the mindset only matters if it changes what you do.
If you are a parent, change your praise, starting with the very next thing your child does well.

Bite back the automatic you are so smart, you are a natural, and instead say what you actually noticed about how they did it: you found a really clever way to solve that, or, you kept going even when it got frustrating, and look what happened, or, I love how you tried three different approaches.
When your child fails or struggles, resist the urge to reassure them that they are brilliant and it does not matter.
Get curious with them about what they could try differently, and treat the struggle as the interesting part.
Add the word yet to their vocabulary and yours: not I am bad at math, but I am not good at math yet.
And model it.
Let your kids see you take on hard things, fumble, learn, and stick with it, because they are learning mindset from watching you at least as much as from your words.
Be careful, too, not to communicate that your love and approval ride on their performance, because a child who feels they are only valued when they win learns to fear every contest.
If you are a manager or a leader, the change is to stop running your team as a talent show and start running it as a learning organization.
Praise and reward process, growth, collaboration, and smart risk taking, not just raw results and not the myth of the star genius.

Make it not just safe but expected to say I do not know, I was wrong, I need help, and I made a mistake, and model those words yourself first, because your people watch what you do, not what your values poster says.

Like Welch, pay attention to how results are achieved, and do not tolerate people who deliver numbers while poisoning the culture, because that teaches everyone that image and results are all that matter and honesty is for suckers.

Actively hunt for the bad news, invite dissent, protect the person who raises the uncomfortable problem, and treat setbacks as data rather than as someone to blame, because a team that is punished for mistakes will simply hide them until they are catastrophes.

Hire for the capacity and hunger to grow, not only for the polished pedigree, and then actually develop people, because you will get more out of a person who believes they can grow than out of a person defending a reputation.
And if you are simply a person trying to live better, which is all of us, the change is quieter but maybe the most important.

Start listening for your own fixed mindset voice, and get to know the specific situations that trigger it, the criticism that makes you defensive, the challenge that makes you want to hide, the other person's success that makes you feel small.
When it shows up, name it, remember that its story is a choice and not the truth, and answer it with the growth mindset.

Then take one growth mindset action, the thing the fixed voice is telling you to avoid: apply for the stretch role, ask for the honest feedback, sign up for the class you think you will be bad at, start the hard conversation, go back to the rejected work and make it better.
Redefine, for yourself, what success and failure even mean.
Stop measuring success by how effortlessly brilliant you looked and how carefully you avoided any risk of looking foolish.
Start measuring it by whether you stretched, whether you learned, whether you did something today that you could not do yesterday.
Change what you feel proud of, from I did not have to try to I tried something hard and I am growing.
Because in the end, that is the quiet revolution this book is asking for.

The fixed mindset makes your life a series of exams you have to keep passing to prove you are worthy, and it makes every hard thing a threat, every effort an admission of weakness, every setback a verdict, every other person's success a measure of your failure.
It is an exhausting way to live, and worse, it holds you back from the very growth that would make you who you want to be.
The growth mindset offers you something better and, honestly, kinder.
It says your qualities are not a fixed hand you have to keep bluffing with.
They are a garden you get to tend.
Your intelligence can grow.
Your abilities can grow.
Your relationships can grow.
Your character can grow.
Not infinitely, not without limit, not into someone you are not, but far, far beyond wherever you happen to be standing today.

And the price of admission is only this: the willingness to try hard things, to learn from the people and the failures around you, and to add one small word, yet, to every place where you are tempted to say you cannot.

So the next time you are handed a puzzle that is a little too hard, and you feel that flush of dread, that voice that says maybe you are just not good enough, see if you can do what a certain ten year old did, all those years ago, in a room full of researchers who were about to have their minds changed.
See if you can rub your hands together, lean in, and say, honestly, I love a challenge.
I was hoping this would be informative.
Because it will be.
That is the whole secret.
It always, always is.
